title: Federal Judge Finds ICE Used 'Unconstitutional Policies' of Racial Profiling in Minnesota
excerpt: In a 111-page ruling, U.S. District Judge Eric Tostrud found that ICE and CBP agents likely followed 'unconstitutional policies' during Operation Metro Surge, stopping and detaining as many as 23 people based solely on race or ethnicity in violation of the Fourth Amendment.
content:
On March 9, 2026, U.S. District Judge Eric Tostrud issued a 111-page ruling in the ACLU's class-action lawsuit challenging ICE and CBP enforcement practices during Operation Metro Surge in Minnesota.

The judge found that plaintiffs had made a "clear showing" that federal agents adopted unlawful and unconstitutional policies — stopping and arresting Minnesotans in violation of their Fourth Amendment rights. He wrote that as many as 23 immigrants appeared to have been stopped or "questioned about their immigration status based solely on their race or ethnicity."

  • Testimony detailed specific incidents: one Somali-American U.S. citizen, Mubashir Hussen, 20, was detained, shackled, and fingerprinted after repeatedly stating he was a citizen; agents refused to look at his ID.
  • Another plaintiff testified that an agent told him his citizenship status "didn't matter" during a detention.
  • Despite the finding of likely unconstitutional conduct, the judge declined to issue a preliminary injunction, citing the drawdown of federal agents from Minnesota and finding the plaintiffs were no longer at immediate risk.
  • The ACLU called the ruling an "important step" toward accountability and said it would continue pursuing a permanent ruling on the legality of the policies.
  • DHS denied all allegations of racial profiling, calling such accusations "disgusting, reckless, and categorically FALSE."
